title: practical english usage - Part 61
content:
grammar •  68  modals: grammar, pronunciation and contractions standard British English and /kænt/ in standard American English.

pronounced /'ʃodə/,  /'kodə/  and / maitə/. Can't is pronounced /kɑ:nt/ in in conversational speech; should have, could have and might have are often weak pronunciations, >315. Ought  to is generally pronounced /'ɔ:tə/ ɛoughtaɜ shall /fe\/, should /ʃəd/,    can /kɔn/, could /kəd/,   must /məst/. For more about Several modals have weak pronunciations, used when they are not stressed: 2 pronunciation          and   contractions There may be rain later today.

when these are followed by be (►  605).

f  There is quite often used as a preparatory subject with modal verbs, especially For details of these uses, see the entries on particular modal verbs.

I think I may have annoyed Aunt Mary.

You should have told me you were coming.

infinitive (have + past participle).

e  Certain past ideas can be expressed by a modal verb followed by a perfect they can sometimes be used as past tenses of can, may, will and shall.

Could, might, would and should usually have present and future reference, but work . ..) not People really had to work hard in those days, (     People really musted not I'd like to be able to skate, (     ... to can skate.) when necessary.

exist), and they do not normally have past forms. Other expressions are used d  Modal  verbs do not have infinitives or participles (temtayr-maymgr-m&yed do not The kitchen ought to be painted one of these days.

She was so angry she could have killed him.

I may not be working tomorrow.

Progressive, perfect and passive infinitives are also possible (►  89).

/ must water the flowers, (     I must to wate^. . .) is an exception, ►    70.1.

c   After modal auxiliary verbs, we  use the infinitive without to of other verbs. Ought not He shouldn't he doing that, should he? (     Hc-doesn't-should-...) not 'Can you swim? (     Do-you-can-surim)' 'Yes, I can.’ b  Questions, negatives, tags and short answers are made without do.

She may know his address, (     She-m&ys-...) a  Modal  verbs have no -s in the third person singular.

Modal  verbs differ from ordinary verbs in several ways.

68 modals:     grammar,      pronunciation          and grammar •  69  deduction (deciding that something is certain): must, can’ t, etc Where can Jack have put the matches? He can’ t have thrown them away. -» Can is used in questions and negatives.

'A woman phoned while you were out.’ 'It must  have been Kate.’ 'We went to Rome last month.’ 'That must  have been nice.’ We   can use must  have + past participle to express certainty about the past.

4 That     must      have      been      nice.

or                                                                       not (   ... It doesn't have to be...) (     ... #  mustn ’t be...) 'Look at those tracks. That must  be a dog’ 'It needn’ t be - it could be a fox.’ sense.

not necessarily so; does not have to can also be used. Must not is not used in this Need not / needn't is used (especially in British English) to say that something is 3 need      not    / does      not    have      to there is evidence that something is not the case (►   69.8 below).

And must not is occasionally used, especially in American English, to say that not It must  be nice to be a cat, mustn't it? (     . .. -ean^t-it?) British English.

However, mustn ’t is used in question tags (►   305-306) after must, especially in She’s not answering the phone. She can’ t be at home.

It can’ t be Emily at the door. She’s in Ireland, (     It mustn ’t be Emily-...) If A is bigger than B, and B is bigger than C, then C can’ t be bigger than A.

logically or practically impossible, or extremely improbable.

cannot/can’t to say that something is certainly not the case, because it is Must is not often used to express certainty in negative clauses. We   normally use 2 negatives:      It can't     be   Emily.

Only five o’clock? It’s got to be later than that!

Getting married next week? You have to be joking.

There’s the doorbell. It has to be George.
